sections.plain_summary: Bile acid malabsorption (BAM) is a condition in which bile acids — digestive fluids made by the liver and released into the small intestine — are not properly reabsorbed at the end of the small bowel. Instead, they spill into the colon, where they trigger a rush of fluid and cause urgent, watery, often painful diarrhoea. It is one of the most commonly missed causes of chronic diarrhoea, frequently misdiagnosed as IBS-D for years.
sections.detailed_summary:
Bile acids are produced by the liver, stored in the gallbladder, and released into the small intestine after eating to help digest fats. Under normal circumstances, about 95% of bile acids are reabsorbed in the terminal ileum (the last segment of the small intestine) and recycled back to the liver. When this reabsorption fails — for any reason — excess bile acids reach the colon. There, they stimulate water secretion and accelerate colonic transit, producing the watery, urgent, often explosive diarrhoea that characterises BAM.

BAM is classified into three types. Type 1 occurs when the terminal ileum is damaged or surgically removed — most commonly in Crohn's disease or after right hemicolectomy. Type 2 is idiopathic (primary BAM), in which no structural cause is found but there appears to be impaired production of FGF19, a hormone that normally signals the liver to reduce bile acid synthesis. Type 3 is secondary to a range of other conditions including cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal), microscopic colitis, coeliac disease, chronic pancreatitis, and post-infectious gut changes.

BAM is substantially underdiagnosed. Studies suggest it may account for up to one-third of people labelled with IBS-D or functional diarrhoea. The gold-standard diagnostic test — the SeHCAT scan — is unavailable in many countries including the United States. This has led to widespread under-detection. A pragmatic therapeutic trial with a bile acid sequestrant (cholestyramine, colestipol, or colesevelam) is often used as a diagnostic tool in the absence of formal testing.

Most people with BAM respond well to treatment once correctly identified. A low-fat diet reduces the stimulus for bile acid release and can meaningfully reduce symptom severity. Bile acid sequestrant medications bind excess bile acids in the gut before they reach the colon. For many people, the combination of dietary modification and medication produces significant or complete symptom relief — which makes the years-long delay to diagnosis particularly frustrating.

prevalence.summary: More common than most clinicians realise — likely affects a significant minority of people diagnosed with IBS-D or functional diarrhoea.
prevalence.estimate: Estimated to affect 1–2% of the general population; up to 25–35% of those diagnosed with IBS-D or functional diarrhoea
prevalence.notes: Prevalence estimates vary widely because most countries lack access to the SeHCAT diagnostic test, leading to systematic under-detection. A 2015 UK study found BAM in approximately 32% of people referred for investigation of chronic diarrhoea. The idiopathic (Type 2) form may be more common in women. Post-cholecystectomy BAM is increasingly recognised as the gallbladder removal rate rises.

subgroups.name: Type 1 — Ileal disease or resection
subgroups.description: Occurs when the terminal ileum is structurally damaged or surgically removed, destroying the anatomical site of bile acid reabsorption.
distinguishing_features: History of Crohn's disease involving the ileum, right hemicolectomy, or bowel resection
Diarrhoea often begins or worsens after surgery
May coexist with fat malabsorption and vitamin B12 deficiency due to wider ileal dysfunction
Steatorrhoea (fatty stools) may be more prominent than in other types

subgroups.name: Type 2 — Idiopathic (Primary BAM)
subgroups.description: No structural or secondary cause is found. The underlying mechanism is thought to involve insufficient production of FGF19, a gut hormone that signals the liver to downregulate bile acid synthesis, resulting in chronic overproduction.
distinguishing_features: No prior bowel surgery, inflammatory bowel disease, or obvious secondary cause
Often misdiagnosed as IBS-D for years
Low serum FGF19 levels may be detectable on specialist testing
Tends to present in middle-aged adults; possible female predominance
Usually responds well to bile acid sequestrants

subgroups.name: Type 3 — Secondary to other conditions
subgroups.description: BAM arises as a downstream consequence of another condition or procedure that disrupts bile acid metabolism or enterohepatic circulation.
distinguishing_features: Associated triggers include cholecystectomy, coeliac disease, microscopic colitis, chronic pancreatitis, radiation enteritis, and post-infectious gut damage
Diarrhoea may begin shortly after gallbladder removal or onset of the primary condition
Treating the underlying condition may partially or fully resolve BAM
Post-cholecystectomy BAM may involve continuous bile drip into the duodenum rather than regulated release

subgroups.name: Post-infectious BAM
subgroups.description: A subset within Type 3 in which bile acid malabsorption develops following a significant gastrointestinal infection, likely due to temporary or lasting damage to ileal transporters.
distinguishing_features: Chronic diarrhoea beginning within weeks of a gut infection (bacterial, viral, or parasitic)
Can be difficult to distinguish from post-infectious IBS without testing
May partially resolve over months as the gut heals, or may persist long-term

symptoms.notes: Often the dominant symptom. Stools are typically loose to liquid, may be pale or yellow, and can be voluminous. Frequently worse in the morning and after eating.
Bile acids accelerate colonic transit. Many people experience sudden, difficult-to-defer urgency. Incontinence can occur and significantly affects quality of life.
Often felt before or during a bowel movement, usually in the lower abdomen. May be severe and crampy rather than constant.
Bile acids in the colon alter fermentation patterns and gas production. Bloating is common but typically less prominent than diarrhoea.
More common in Type 1 BAM where fat malabsorption coexists. Stools may float. Indicates fat is reaching the colon undigested.

symptoms.symptom: Diarrhoea worsened by fatty or large meals
symptoms.notes: Fat in the duodenum is the primary trigger for gallbladder contraction and bile release. Meals high in fat tend to provoke the worst episodes.
May accompany acute episodes, particularly after eating.
Can result from disrupted sleep due to nocturnal diarrhoea, nutritional deficiencies, or the ongoing physiological stress of chronic malabsorption.

symptoms.symptom: Weight loss or difficulty maintaining weight
symptoms.notes: More prominent in Type 1 BAM. Impaired fat absorption reduces caloric intake. Fear of eating may also reduce food intake.

Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) may be depleted if fat malabsorption is significant. Vitamin B12 deficiency is common in ileal disease. Deficiencies may cause fatigue, bone pain, or neurological symptoms over time.
Waking from sleep with urgent diarrhoea is a feature that clinicians often use to distinguish organic causes (like BAM) from purely functional conditions.
Bile acids in stool are chemically irritating to perianal skin. Frequent, loose stools can cause significant discomfort, redness, or skin breakdown.

key_patterns: Chronic watery diarrhoea persisting for more than 4 weeks that has not responded to a low-FODMAP diet or IBS treatments
Diarrhoea that began after cholecystectomy, bowel resection, or a significant gut infection
Yellow or pale stools with urgency and cramping, worse after fatty meals
IBS-D diagnosis made without investigation of bile acid status
Nocturnal diarrhoea or faecal urgency suggesting organic rather than functional cause
Coexisting Crohn's disease, coeliac disease, or microscopic colitis alongside chronic diarrhoea

at_home.name: Low-fat diet trial
at_home.description: Reducing dietary fat to approximately 40g per day for 1–2 weeks to observe whether diarrhoea frequency and urgency decrease. Fat stimulates bile release, so reducing it lowers the bile acid load reaching the colon. Keep a symptom diary to track changes in stool consistency (using the Bristol Stool Scale), urgency, and frequency.
at_home.safety_notes: A short-term low-fat trial is generally safe for most people. If fat intake is already very low or weight loss is a concern, consult a dietitian before restricting further. A trial period of only 2 weeks is sufficient to identify a signal.

at_home.name: Symptom and food diary
at_home.description: Recording meals, fat content, stool type (Bristol scale 1–7), urgency level, and timing of episodes over 2–4 weeks. This helps identify patterns linking fat intake and symptom severity, and provides useful data for any clinician or dietitian consultation.
at_home.safety_notes: No safety concerns. The diary itself has no risk; it is an observation tool.

at_home.name: Bile acid sequestrant trial (with medical oversight)
at_home.description: In countries where SeHCAT scanning is unavailable, a short empirical trial of a bile acid sequestrant (such as cholestyramine) is widely used as both a diagnostic and therapeutic test. A positive response — marked reduction in diarrhoea within 1–2 weeks — is considered strong diagnostic evidence. This requires a clinician to prescribe the medication, but the trial itself can be planned and requested by an informed patient.
at_home.safety_notes: Bile acid sequestrants require a prescription in most countries. They can interfere with absorption of other medications and should be taken 1–2 hours apart from other drugs. Do not self-medicate. This entry documents the test strategy, not an instruction to obtain medication without medical involvement.

clinical.name: SeHCAT scan (75Se-homocholic acid taurine retention test)
clinical.description: A nuclear medicine test in which the patient swallows a synthetic radiolabelled bile acid capsule. A whole-body scan is performed after 7 days to measure how much of the labelled bile acid has been retained. Retention below 15% indicates BAM; below 10% suggests moderate-to-severe BAM; below 5% indicates severe BAM. The test is highly sensitive and specific.
clinical.availability: Available in the UK, Scandinavia, and parts of Europe via gastroenterology or nuclear medicine departments. Not available in the United States or many other countries. In the UK, can be accessed via NHS referral from a gastroenterologist. Private referral is also possible. In countries without access, empirical sequestrant trials or serum C4 testing are used instead.

clinical.name: Serum 7α-hydroxy-4-cholesten-3-one (C4 or 7αC4)
clinical.description: A blood test measuring C4, an intermediate in bile acid synthesis. Elevated C4 levels indicate excessive bile acid production, which is the upstream driver of BAM in most cases. Less invasive than SeHCAT and does not require nuclear medicine facilities.
clinical.availability: Available in specialist centres and some academic hospitals. Not universally offered in routine gastroenterology. In the UK, increasingly used as an alternative where SeHCAT is not available. May need to be requested specifically from a gastroenterologist or lipid specialist.

clinical.name: Serum FGF19
clinical.description: FGF19 is the intestinal hormone that normally suppresses hepatic bile acid synthesis. Low FGF19 is associated with Type 2 (idiopathic) BAM and may be detectable with a fasting blood test. Not yet in routine clinical use but may be available at specialist or research centres.
clinical.availability: Research and tertiary centre use primarily. Not widely available in routine NHS or private gastroenterology practice. May be available as part of specialist BAM clinics or research studies.

clinical.name: Faecal bile acid measurement
clinical.description: Direct measurement of bile acids in a stool sample. Elevated faecal bile acids confirm excess colonic bile acid load. Requires specialised laboratory analysis and is not routinely available, but is used in some research and tertiary settings.
clinical.availability: Limited to specialist or research laboratories. Not widely available in routine clinical practice.

clinical.name: Empirical therapeutic trial with bile acid sequestrant
clinical.description: When SeHCAT or C4 testing is unavailable, a 2–4 week trial of cholestyramine, colestipol, or colesevelam is used both diagnostically and therapeutically. A clear reduction in diarrhoea is considered strong supportive evidence for BAM. Colesevelam is better tolerated and has fewer drug interactions than older sequestrants.
clinical.availability: Bile acid sequestrants are widely available by prescription from a general practitioner or gastroenterologist. The trial approach is accepted in UK NICE guidance and international gastroenterology practice as an alternative when formal testing is inaccessible.

clinical.name: Colonoscopy with biopsies
clinical.description: Used to exclude other causes of chronic diarrhoea including inflammatory bowel disease, microscopic colitis, and colorectal cancer. Not diagnostic for BAM itself but an important part of the investigative workup.
clinical.availability: Standard gastroenterology referral pathway. Available via NHS or private gastroenterology.

clinical.name: Coeliac serology and small bowel biopsy
clinical.description: Coeliac disease can cause secondary BAM via ileal damage. Testing for coeliac disease is an important step in the workup, especially if there are additional symptoms such as anaemia or weight loss.
clinical.availability: Coeliac serology (tTG-IgA) available via GP. Small bowel biopsy via gastroenterology referral.

related_conditions.name: IBS-D (diarrhoea-predominant IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: BAM is one of the most common organic conditions misdiagnosed as IBS-D. Studies suggest that up to one-third of people labelled with IBS-D may actually have BAM. The two conditions share overlapping symptoms but have different mechanisms and different treatments. IBS is a diagnosis of exclusion; BAM should be ruled out before that label is applied.

related_conditions.name: Crohn's disease
related_conditions.explanation: Crohn's disease affecting the terminal ileum is the most common cause of Type 1 BAM. Inflammation or surgical removal of the ileum destroys the site where bile acids are normally reabsorbed, causing them to spill into the colon.

related_conditions.name: Microscopic colitis
related_conditions.explanation: Microscopic colitis and BAM frequently coexist, and both can cause chronic watery diarrhoea. BAM has been found in a significant proportion of people with microscopic colitis. The relationship may be mechanistic — bile acids themselves may contribute to colonic inflammation.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease can damage the small intestinal mucosa, impairing ileal bile acid transporters and causing secondary BAM. People with coeliac disease who have persistent diarrhoea despite a gluten-free diet may warrant investigation for BAM.

related_conditions.name: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency
related_conditions.explanation: Both EPI and BAM cause chronic diarrhoea, fatty stools, and weight loss. They can coexist and are often confused. Distinguishing them matters because treatments differ — EPI requires pancreatic enzyme replacement, while BAM requires bile acid sequestrants. A faecal elastase test can screen for EPI.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO can occur as a comorbidity in people with structural changes (such as post-surgical anatomy) that also predispose to BAM. Excess bacteria in the small intestine can deconjugate bile acids, worsening malabsorption and diarrhoea. Both conditions may need to be addressed for symptoms to improve.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Excess bile acids in the colon alter the gut microbiome, favouring bile-resistant bacteria and reducing microbial diversity. This dysbiosis may perpetuate symptoms and contribute to colonic inflammation even in treated BAM.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Upper GI symptoms including nausea, early satiety, and bloating are sometimes present alongside BAM, particularly where gallbladder dysfunction or altered bile flow is involved.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism can accelerate gut transit and cause chronic diarrhoea, mimicking BAM. Thyroid function is often checked as part of the workup for unexplained chronic diarrhoea.

related_conditions.name: Cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal)
related_conditions.explanation: Removal of the gallbladder eliminates the reservoir function that normally concentrates and releases bile in response to meals. Instead, bile drips continuously into the duodenum. This continuous delivery disrupts the normal enterohepatic cycle and commonly leads to Type 3 BAM. Post-cholecystectomy diarrhoea affects a meaningful proportion of people who undergo the procedure.

related_conditions.name: Short bowel syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Extensive small bowel resection, particularly involving the ileum, results in severe BAM as the primary reabsorption site is removed. BAM in this context is often a major driver of nutritional compromise.

related_conditions.name: Radiation enteritis
related_conditions.explanation: Pelvic or abdominal radiotherapy can damage ileal mucosa and transporters, impairing bile acid reabsorption and causing secondary BAM, sometimes years after treatment.

related_conditions.name: Oxalate nephropathy / hyperoxaluria
related_conditions.explanation: In BAM with significant fat malabsorption, unabsorbed fat binds calcium in the gut, leaving oxalate free to be absorbed in high quantities. This enteric hyperoxaluria increases the risk of calcium oxalate kidney stones — an underappreciated complication of severe or longstanding BAM.

related_conditions.name: Osteoporosis / osteopenia
related_conditions.explanation: Impaired fat-soluble vitamin absorption — particularly vitamin D — can reduce calcium absorption over time, contributing to reduced bone density in people with longstanding, untreated BAM.

strategies.name: Bile acid sequestrant medication
strategies.description: Cholestyramine, colestipol, and colesevelam are the primary treatments for BAM. These medications bind bile acids in the gut lumen, preventing them from reaching the colon. Colesevelam (Cholestagel/Welchol) is generally better tolerated than older powdered sequestrants and has fewer drug interactions. Cholestyramine powder is effective but has a gritty texture and can cause nausea. Response is often seen within 1–2 weeks of starting the correct dose.
strategies.notes: All bile acid sequestrants can impair absorption of other medications. They should be taken 1–4 hours away from other drugs, particularly levothyroxine, warfarin, and fat-soluble vitamin supplements. Dose titration is often needed to balance symptom control against constipation as a side effect. Prescribed by a GP or gastroenterologist.

strategies.name: Low-fat diet
strategies.description: Reducing dietary fat reduces the stimulus for bile acid release, lowering the total bile acid load that reaches the terminal ileum and colon. Many clinicians recommend keeping fat intake to 40g or less per day as a starting point. This can substantially reduce frequency and urgency for many people, particularly those with more severe BAM.
strategies.notes: A low-fat diet alone is rarely sufficient to fully control BAM, but it can meaningfully reduce the medication dose required and improve quality of life. Working with a registered dietitian experienced in malabsorption conditions is strongly recommended to ensure nutritional adequacy, particularly for fat-soluble vitamins and essential fatty acids.

strategies.name: Registered dietitian support
strategies.description: A dietitian with experience in malabsorption or gastroenterology can help design a low-fat eating plan that remains nutritionally complete, assess for vitamin deficiencies, advise on practical meal planning, and help identify any additional food sensitivities that may be compounding symptoms.
strategies.notes: Not all dietitians are experienced in BAM specifically. It is worth seeking one with a background in malabsorption, IBD, or functional gut disorders. The British Dietetic Association and similar bodies maintain searchable directories.

strategies.name: Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation
strategies.description: People with significant BAM, especially Type 1, may develop deficiencies in vitamins A, D, E, and K due to impaired fat absorption. Vitamin D deficiency is particularly common and may contribute to fatigue, bone pain, and mood changes. Supplementation guided by blood testing is important in moderate-to-severe BAM.
strategies.notes: Supplementation should be guided by blood levels rather than assumed. Vitamin D, B12 (especially in ileal disease), magnesium, and zinc are the most commonly depleted. Bile acid sequestrants themselves can worsen fat-soluble vitamin absorption, making monitoring even more important in people taking these medications.

strategies.name: Vitamin B12 supplementation or injections
strategies.description: In Type 1 BAM with ileal disease or resection, vitamin B12 absorption may be significantly impaired because intrinsic factor-B12 complexes are absorbed specifically in the terminal ileum. Oral supplementation may be insufficient in these cases; intramuscular injections bypass the ileum entirely.
strategies.notes: Relevant primarily in Type 1 BAM. B12 deficiency can cause fatigue, peripheral neuropathy, and cognitive difficulties. Blood levels should be monitored regularly.

strategies.name: Meal size and frequency adjustment
strategies.description: Eating smaller, more frequent meals rather than large ones reduces the bile acid surge triggered by any single meal. Avoiding very large or high-fat meals, especially late in the day, can reduce overnight urgency and morning diarrhoea.
strategies.notes: Practical lifestyle adjustment rather than a standalone treatment. Works best alongside dietary fat reduction and medication.

strategies.name: Treating the underlying condition
strategies.description: In Type 1 and Type 3 BAM, addressing the underlying driver — e.g. achieving remission in Crohn's disease, treating coeliac disease with a strict gluten-free diet, or managing microscopic colitis — may improve or partially resolve BAM. Secondary BAM may be reversible if the primary cause is adequately controlled.
strategies.notes: Does not apply to idiopathic (Type 2) BAM where no underlying cause is identifiable. Even when an underlying condition is treated, residual BAM may persist and require sequestrant therapy.

strategies.name: Loperamide as adjunct
strategies.description: Loperamide (Imodium) can be used on an as-needed basis to reduce stool urgency and frequency, particularly for situations where predictability is important (travel, work, social events). It slows gut transit but does not address the underlying bile acid excess.
strategies.notes: Useful as an adjunct rather than a primary treatment. Should not be used to mask symptoms without understanding the underlying diagnosis. Regular daily use without a confirmed BAM diagnosis or management plan is not ideal.

things_to_avoid: High-fat meals — fat is the primary trigger for gallbladder contraction and bile release, directly worsening symptoms
Fatty or greasy takeaway foods, deep-fried foods, full-fat dairy in large quantities, and fatty meats — these provoke the strongest bile acid surges
Taking other medications at the same time as bile acid sequestrants — sequestrants can bind and impair absorption of many drugs; always take them separately
Assuming a low-FODMAP diet will resolve BAM — FODMAP restriction does not address bile acid excess and may provide minimal benefit, delaying correct treatment
Ignoring fat-soluble vitamin status — longstanding BAM without monitoring can lead to clinically significant deficiencies that cause independent health problems
Accepting an IBS-D diagnosis without asking whether BAM has been excluded — particularly if there is a history of cholecystectomy, ileal surgery, or bowel disease
Rapidly increasing bile acid sequestrant dose without titration — too high a dose can cause rebound constipation and nutrient impairment

uncomfortable_truths.truth: BAM is frequently misdiagnosed as IBS-D for years, and most mainstream clinicians will not test for it unless directly prompted. People often have to advocate specifically for SeHCAT scanning or a sequestrant trial.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients trust that if there were a treatable cause, it would have been found. The IBS label feels authoritative and final. Challenging a diagnosis requires confidence and knowledge that most people do not initially have.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who research their own symptom pattern, identify BAM as a plausible explanation, and request a therapeutic trial or SeHCAT referral often achieve rapid, significant symptom relief after years of inadequate management. The diagnosis is not hard to pursue — it requires asking the right questions of the right clinician.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The gold-standard diagnostic test (SeHCAT) is unavailable in the United States and many other countries, meaning BAM can be structurally invisible to the medical system regardless of how clearly it presents.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People assume that if a definitive test existed, it would be available to them. The absence of a test in their country feels like absence of the condition.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: In countries without SeHCAT, a pragmatic trial with a bile acid sequestrant is an accepted diagnostic approach. A clear, rapid response to cholestyramine or colesevelam is itself strong evidence of BAM. Requesting an empirical trial from a gastroenterologist or informed GP is a legitimate and productive path forward.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: For many people, BAM requires ongoing management rather than cure. A low-fat diet and daily medication may need to be maintained indefinitely, particularly in idiopathic or structural BAM.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The hope that a single intervention will restore normality is natural. Accepting a long-term management framework can feel like admitting defeat.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who accept the maintenance nature of BAM treatment and establish a consistent dietary and medication routine typically report dramatically improved quality of life — often returning to near-normal social, occupational, and physical function within weeks to months of starting treatment.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Post-cholecystectomy BAM is a recognised and relatively common complication of gallbladder removal that is almost never discussed with patients beforehand — and is frequently not investigated afterwards, even when diarrhoea begins directly after surgery.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Surgeons focus on the operation, not long-term GI consequences. Patients are relieved to have a 'simple' procedure and may not connect subsequent diarrhoea to the surgery. GPs may not think to investigate this link.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who connect the onset of their diarrhoea to their cholecystectomy and present this timeline to a gastroenterologist with a specific request for BAM investigation tend to be taken seriously and often reach a useful treatment within a manageable timeframe.

key_levers.lever: Correct diagnosis confirmed by SeHCAT, C4, or sequestrant trial
key_levers.why_it_matters: BAM is highly treatable but only once identified. Years spent managing the wrong diagnosis (IBS-D) with ineffective interventions can be avoided once BAM is confirmed. The diagnosis unlocks appropriate, targeted treatment.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a gastroenterology referral with specific mention of BAM as a suspected cause. Ask whether SeHCAT scanning or a C4 blood test is available, or request an empirical cholestyramine/colesevelam trial if scanning is not accessible.
key_levers.timeline: Referral timelines vary. An empirical medication trial can begin within days of prescription and typically produces observable results within 1–2 weeks.

key_levers.lever: Bile acid sequestrant at an optimal individualised dose
key_levers.why_it_matters: The right sequestrant at the right dose is the most powerful single intervention for BAM. Underdosing produces partial relief; overdosing causes constipation. Titration to find the minimum effective dose takes time but results in the best long-term outcomes.
key_levers.how_to_start: Start at the lowest recommended dose (e.g. 4g cholestyramine once daily or 625mg colesevelam tablets) and increase gradually over 2–4 weeks guided by symptoms. Keep a symptom diary to track stool frequency, urgency, and consistency.
key_levers.timeline: Initial response often within 1–2 weeks. Optimal titration may take 4–8 weeks.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Reducing fat intake lowers the bile acid stimulus from every meal. This allows the same dose of sequestrant to work more effectively, and some people can reduce medication once dietary fat is consistently controlled.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with a registered dietitian or use reliable resources to audit current dietary fat intake. Aim to keep total fat below 40g/day as a starting point, with particular attention to high-fat single meals.
key_levers.timeline: Dietary changes typically show symptom impact within 1–2 weeks of consistent adherence.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Unaddressed vitamin and mineral deficiencies — particularly vitamins D, B12, and fat-soluble vitamins — can cause independent symptoms (fatigue, bone pain, neurological symptoms) that persist even after diarrhoea is controlled. These are often overlooked once GI symptoms improve.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a comprehensive blood panel including vitamin D, B12, folate, magnesium, zinc, and full blood count. Ask specifically whether fat-soluble vitamins should be tested given the diagnosis.
key_levers.timeline: Deficiency correction typically takes 3–6 months of consistent supplementation to normalise blood levels.

key_levers.lever: Treating an underlying condition driving Type 1 or Type 3 BAM
key_levers.why_it_matters: When BAM is secondary, controlling the primary condition can reduce the severity of BAM and sometimes allow a reduction in sequestrant dose. Ignoring the underlying condition while only treating BAM symptoms leads to ongoing structural damage.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ensure that conditions such as Crohn's disease, coeliac disease, or microscopic colitis are under active, appropriate management in parallel with BAM treatment.
key_levers.timeline: Depends on the underlying condition. Some improvements in secondary BAM may take months.

common_mistakes: Trying a low-FODMAP diet as the primary intervention without investigating bile acid status — FODMAP restriction does not address bile acid excess and often provides little benefit in BAM

Stopping bile acid sequestrants early because of initial side effects (bloating, constipation) without adjusting dose or formulation — titration matters and switching between sequestrants may resolve tolerability issues
Taking bile acid sequestrants at the same time as other medications, reducing their absorption and reducing the benefit of both
Not following up on nutritional status once diarrhoea is controlled — vitamin deficiencies accumulate silently and may cause serious longer-term health consequences
Accepting an IBS-D diagnosis without pushing for BAM investigation, particularly after cholecystectomy, bowel surgery, or known ileal disease
Assuming BAM has been ruled out when no SeHCAT test has been performed — absence of testing is not the same as a negative result
Eating large, high-fat meals even while on medication — sequestrants have a limited capacity and a single large fatty meal can overwhelm their binding capacity

specialists.role: The primary specialist for BAM investigation and diagnosis. Can order SeHCAT scanning, C4 blood tests, colonoscopy, and other investigations. Can also prescribe bile acid sequestrants and manage underlying conditions such as Crohn's or microscopic colitis.
specialists.notes: Not all gastroenterologists routinely investigate for BAM; many default to IBS-D management. Patients who present with a specific request for BAM investigation — especially with a relevant history such as cholecystectomy, ileal disease, or failure of IBS treatments — are more likely to have testing pursued. In the UK, NHS referral via GP is the standard route. Private gastroenterology can reduce waiting times.

specialists.role: Essential for designing a low-fat diet that remains nutritionally complete, assessing for and addressing vitamin deficiencies, and providing practical meal planning support. Also helpful in distinguishing dietary triggers from structural causes.
specialists.notes: Seek a dietitian with experience in malabsorption, gastroenterology, or inflammatory bowel conditions. General practice dietitians may have limited experience with BAM specifically. In the UK, the British Dietetic Association's 'Find a Dietitian' tool is a useful starting point. Private dietitian access is typically faster than NHS referral.

specialists.role: First point of contact for referral to gastroenterology, prescription of bile acid sequestrants, and ordering basic blood tests (full blood count, vitamin D, B12, thyroid function, coeliac serology). A well-informed GP can initiate an empirical sequestrant trial without waiting for a specialist appointment.
specialists.notes: Awareness of BAM varies widely among GPs. A patient who presents with specific knowledge of their symptom pattern and a clear request (e.g. 'I would like to try a cholestyramine trial given my post-cholecystectomy diarrhoea') is more likely to receive a useful response than one who presents with general diarrhoea complaints.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative gastroenterologist
specialists.role: May be more willing to investigate BAM comprehensively when conventional pathways have failed to provide a diagnosis. Can sometimes order FGF19 testing, comprehensive microbiome assessments, and nutritional panels that mainstream clinicians may not prioritise.
specialists.notes: Quality varies considerably. Seek practitioners with verifiable training and a transparent evidence base. May be a useful option when NHS or standard private gastroenterology has not yielded a diagnosis after investigation.

patient_communities.name: r/BileAcidMalabsorption
patient_communities.notes: A dedicated community for people with BAM. Useful for peer experience on diagnosis pathways, medication tolerability, dietary strategies, and navigating healthcare systems in different countries. Particularly valuable for those in countries without SeHCAT access who want to understand empirical trial approaches.

patient_communities.name: r/ibs
patient_communities.notes: A large IBS community where BAM is a frequently discussed topic, given how often the two are confused. Useful for finding others who have pursued a BAM diagnosis after years of IBS-D labelling.

patient_communities.name: Bile Acid Malabsorption support group
patient_communities.notes: Several Facebook groups exist for BAM support. Search 'Bile Acid Malabsorption' on Facebook. These groups tend to have active members sharing practical experience with cholestyramine, colesevelam, dietary management, and getting diagnosed in countries without SeHCAT access.

patient_communities.name: Crohn's and Colitis UK
patient_communities.notes: Useful for people whose BAM is secondary to Crohn's disease. Includes forums, helpline access, and information about managing bile acid complications of ileal disease.

learning_resources.title: Bile acid malabsorption — NICE guidance (CG61 and related guidance)
learning_resources.author_or_source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE)
learning_resources.description: UK national guidance on the investigation and management of diarrhoea-predominant bowel conditions includes recommendation of SeHCAT testing and empirical sequestrant trials for BAM. Provides a useful framework for what investigations should be available and requested.

learning_resources.title: Bile Acid Malabsorption: A Review
learning_resources.author_or_source: Camilleri M — published in Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive clinical review by a leading gastroenterologist summarising the epidemiology, pathophysiology, diagnosis, and treatment of BAM. Accessible to motivated lay readers with some science background. Useful for understanding the evidence base and the current clinical debate.

learning_resources.title: SeHCAT testing for bile acid malabsorption — NICE diagnostics guidance DG7
learning_resources.description: The specific NICE guidance recommending SeHCAT as the investigation of choice for people with chronic diarrhoea where BAM is suspected. Useful as a reference document when requesting testing from a clinician who is unfamiliar with the test.

learning_resources.title: Bile Acid Malabsorption — Patient Information (Guts UK)
learning_resources.description: Clear, patient-friendly overview of BAM covering symptoms, types, testing, and treatment. Written by a UK gastroenterology charity and a good starting point for newly diagnosed people or those seeking a plain-language introduction.

learning_resources.title: The Gut Health Podcast — episodes on bile acids and diarrhoea
learning_resources.author_or_source: Gut Health Podcast (Kirsten Jackson / various gastroenterology guests)
learning_resources.description: Accessible discussions of digestive conditions including BAM. Useful for those who prefer audio content and want to understand the gut biology behind bile acid dysfunction without reading clinical papers.

learning_resources.title: FGF19 and bile acid malabsorption — emerging diagnostics
learning_resources.author_or_source: Walters JRF et al — Alimentary Pharmacology & Therapeutics
learning_resources.description: Key paper establishing the role of FGF19 deficiency in idiopathic BAM and introducing the concept of measuring serum FGF19 as a diagnostic tool. Important background reading for understanding the mechanism of Type 2 BAM.

sections.relevance_signal: Chronic watery diarrhoea — often yellow or pale, greasy, and urgent — that is worse after eating fatty meals, has been present for months or years, and has not responded to IBS treatments or low-FODMAP diet. May follow gallbladder removal, bowel surgery, or a gut infection, or may appear with no obvious cause.
